title: Ethiopia: progress in rural areas
subtitle: Solar electricity is a key factor of progress in rural areas
source: World Bank, Development Data Group . Space2Stats (accessed: February 20, 2026).
visdescription: Map of nighttime lights in Ethiopia as a proxy for electricity access. It focused on access progress in rural areas.
newly_lit_2023: Dark areas in 2012 that had some night lights in 2023

scenes.text: Let’s focus on Ethiopia’s [emphasis: rural areas only][footnote: Urban areas are left blank to focus on the nighttime lights view in rural areas.]and take a look at the nighttime lights view of 2012. The areas where at least some light (weak or strong) was detected are shown in light blue, and areas where no nighttime light was detected, in dark blue. While western parts of rural Ethiopia had some nighttime lights coverage in 2012, eastern and southern rural Ethiopia remained largely in the dark.

The power grid lines reached only a few rural areas in 2012. So how did the illuminated rural areas have access to electricity? About 75 percent of the [emphasis: rural population] in Ethiopia with access consumed electricity through[emphasis: solar power.][reference: ethiopia_survey]
Let’s focus on the [emphasis: south-east,] which is more sparsely populated and remained mostly in the dark in 2012[emphasis: .]

By 2023, a lot more nighttime lights could be detected in the southeast. Much of this is due to the [emphasis: rapid uptake of solar electricity,][reference: ethiopia_survey] which helped reduce the persistent gap in access to electricity in rural areas.
